attrs.alt: Azure service-specific built-in roles across Storage, Key Vault, Compute, and Container services

content.text: Beyond the generic Owner, Contributor, and Reader roles, Azure provides over 600 built-in roles scoped to individual services. These roles embody the principle of least privilege: they grant only the permissions required for a specific task within one service, often targeting the data plane rather than the control plane.

The key distinction is control plane versus data plane. Control-plane operations (ARM) manage the resource itself — creating a storage account, modifying its firewall rules, or rotating its access keys. Data-plane operations act on the data inside the resource — reading a blob, getting a secret, or publishing a message to a queue. Control-plane Contributor access does NOT automatically grant data-plane access; both must be assigned explicitly.
Design principle: assign service-specific data-plane roles to application identities, not broad Contributor or Owner. This limits blast radius if the identity is compromised.

Azure Storage has a complete set of data-plane roles for blobs, queues, and tables. These roles grant access to the data inside storage accounts using Entra ID tokens — no storage account keys or SAS tokens required. Prefer these over shared-key access for all application identities.

# Storage data-plane roles — must be separate from control-plane Contributor
# Contributor grants: manage storage account settings, keys
# Storage Blob Data Contributor grants: read/write/delete blobs via Entra ID

# Grant a service principal blob data access (no shared key needed)
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-data/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorage/blobServices/default/containers/app-data'

# Allow a user to read blobs only (no write, no key access)
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'USER-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-data/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorage'

# Verify access works via Entra ID (not shared key)
$ctx = New-AzStorageContext -StorageAccountName 'mystorage' -UseConnectedAccount
Get-AzStorageBlob -Container 'app-data' -Context $ctx

# Storage Blob Data Owner: includes POSIX ACL operations on ADLS Gen2
# Use this only for data lake administrators who need recursive ACL management
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'DATALAKE-ADMIN-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Owner' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-datalake/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/datalake'

Key Vault supports two permission models: legacy Access Policies and the modern RBAC model. The RBAC model is recommended — it uses standard Azure role assignments, integrates with PIM, and provides fine-grained control with separate roles for secrets, keys, and certificates. Enable the RBAC model on new vaults from the start.

# Key Vault RBAC roles (requires vault in RBAC permission model, not Access Policies)
# Check and switch permission model
Get-AzKeyVault -VaultName 'myvault' | Select-Object EnableRbacAuthorization
Update-AzKeyVault -VaultName 'myvault' -ResourceGroupName 'rg-security' -EnableRbacAuthorization $true

# Grant application identity access to read secrets only
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'APP-MANAGED-IDENTITY-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-security/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault'

# Grant a secrets administrator full secrets lifecycle
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'SECRETS-TEAM-GROUP-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets Officer' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-security/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault'

# Grant HSM-backed key operations (encrypt/decrypt) to an app
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'APP-MANAGED-IDENTITY-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Crypto User' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-security/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault'

# Difference between Crypto Officer and Crypto User:
# Officer: create, import, delete, backup keys — lifecycle management
# User: encrypt, decrypt, sign, verify — operational use only

Virtual machines have roles that separate resource lifecycle management from OS-level access. This allows operations teams to start/stop/resize VMs without gaining login access, and developers to SSH or RDP into VMs without being able to delete them. Combine these roles for the right separation.

# VM-specific roles for Zero Trust VM access

# Enable Azure AD (Entra) login for VM
az vm extension set \
    --resource-group rg-compute \
    --vm-name my-linux-vm \
    --name AADSSHLoginForLinux \
    --publisher Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory

# Grant VM Administrator Login (sudo-equivalent)
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'ADMIN-GROUP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Virtual Machine Administrator Login' \`
    -ResourceGroupName 'rg-compute'

# Grant VM User Login (no sudo)
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'DEV-GROUP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Virtual Machine User Login' \`
    -ResourceGroupName 'rg-compute'

# Virtual Machine Contributor: manage VM lifecycle but NOT login
# Assign to automation/operations teams
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'OPS-SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Virtual Machine Contributor' \`
    -ResourceGroupName 'rg-compute'

# Note: VM Contributor does NOT grant OS-level access.
# Combine with VM Admin/User Login for both plane access.

Container workloads span two services with their own role sets. Azure Container Registry roles control who can push or pull images. AKS RBAC roles (Entra-integrated) control Kubernetes API access — ranging from namespace-scoped writer to full cluster admin. Assign AcrPull to AKS node pools so Kubernetes can pull images without admin credentials.

# Container Registry roles for CI/CD pipelines

# AcrPull: pull images only (read-only; for app runtimes, K8s nodes)
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'AKS-KUBELET-IDENTITY-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'AcrPull' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-containers/providers/Microsoft.ContainerRegistry/registries/myacr'

# AcrPush: push + pull (for build pipelines)
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'BUILD-SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'AcrPush' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-containers/providers/Microsoft.ContainerRegistry/registries/myacr'

# AKS RBAC: cluster-level access control
# Azure Kubernetes Service RBAC Cluster Admin — full cluster admin
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'PLATFORM-TEAM-GROUP-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Azure Kubernetes Service RBAC Cluster Admin' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-k8s/providers/Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters/myaks'

# Azure Kubernetes Service RBAC Writer — deploy workloads, no admin
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'APP-TEAM-GROUP-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Azure Kubernetes Service RBAC Writer' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-k8s/providers/Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters/myaks/namespaces/my-namespace'

Scope AKS RBAC Writer to a specific namespace path (/subscriptions/.../managedClusters/myaks/namespaces/my-namespace) to give teams deployment access to their namespace without touching other teams' workloads.

Monitoring roles let operations and security teams read logs, metrics, alerts, and dashboards without granting broad resource access. Monitoring Reader is safe to assign broadly across subscriptions; Monitoring Contributor is appropriate for SRE teams who create and manage alerts and action groups.

# Monitoring roles for observability teams

# Monitoring Reader: read all monitoring data and settings
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'OPS-GROUP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Monitoring Reader' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB'

# Monitoring Contributor: create/modify alerts, dashboards, action groups
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'OBSERVABILITY-SP-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Monitoring Contributor' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring'

# Log Analytics Reader: query workspaces, read data
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'ANALYST-GROUP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Log Analytics Reader' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-logging/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/myworkspace'

# Log Analytics Contributor: manage workspace settings, linked resources
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'OPS-SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Log Analytics Contributor' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-logging'

# List all monitoring role assignments in a subscription
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Where-Object {
    $_.RoleDefinitionName -match 'Monitoring|Log Analytics|Insights'
} | Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName, Scope
With 600+ built-in roles it is impractical to memorize them all. Use PowerShell or the Azure CLI to discover roles for a specific service before reaching for Contributor.

# List all built-in roles for a service (by namespace prefix)
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object {
    $_.IsCustom -eq $false -and
    $_.Actions -match 'Microsoft.Storage'
} | Select-Object Name, Description | Sort-Object Name

# List roles for Key Vault
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object {
    $_.IsCustom -eq $false -and
    ($_.Name -match 'Key Vault' -or $_.Actions -match 'Microsoft.KeyVault')
} | Select-Object Name | Sort-Object Name

# Inspect exact Actions / DataActions of a candidate role
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty Actions
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty DataActions

# Compare two roles side by side
$a = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Storage Blob Data Reader'
$b = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Storage Blob Data Contributor'
Compare-Object $a.DataActions $b.DataActions

Azure SQL Database, SQL Managed Instance, and Azure Cosmos DB each have service-specific roles. The SQL family separates server-level management (SQL Server Contributor), database-level management (SQL DB Contributor), and security configuration (SQL Security Manager). Cosmos DB has both ARM-level roles and its own built-in data RBAC system.

# Azure SQL / SQL Managed Instance roles
# Control plane: SQL Server Contributor — manage the server resource
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'DBA-GROUP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'SQL Server Contributor' \`
    -ResourceGroupName 'rg-data'

# SQL DB Contributor: manage databases (no server settings, no security)
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'DEV-SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'SQL DB Contributor' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-data/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/myserver/databases/mydb'

# SQL Security Manager: configure auditing, threat detection, vulnerability assessments
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'SECURITY-TEAM-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'SQL Security Manager' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-data/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/myserver'

# Cosmos DB: Account Reader — read account-level settings and documents
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'ANALYST-GROUP-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Cosmos DB Account Reader Role' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-data/providers/Microsoft.DocumentDB/databaseAccounts/mycosmosdb'

# DocumentDB: built-in data-plane RBAC (separate from ARM RBAC)
# Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor: read/write documents in any container
New-AzCosmosDBSqlRoleAssignment \`
    -AccountName 'mycosmosdb' -ResourceGroupName 'rg-data' \`
    -RoleDefinitionId '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-data/providers/Microsoft.DocumentDB/databaseAccounts/mycosmosdb' \`
    -PrincipalId 'APP-MANAGED-IDENTITY-ID'

Azure Service Bus and Azure Event Hubs each expose three data-plane roles: Sender, Receiver, and Owner. Scope these to individual queues, topics, or event hubs rather than the entire namespace to minimize the blast radius if one service principal is compromised.

# Service Bus roles — messaging data plane

# Azure Service Bus Data Sender: send messages to queues/topics
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'PRODUCER-SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Azure Service Bus Data Sender' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-messaging/providers/Microsoft.ServiceBus/namespaces/mybus/queues/order-queue'

# Azure Service Bus Data Receiver: receive and complete messages
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'CONSUMER-SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Azure Service Bus Data Receiver' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-messaging/providers/Microsoft.ServiceBus/namespaces/mybus/queues/order-queue'

# Azure Service Bus Data Owner: full control over namespace data
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'MESSAGING-ADMIN-GROUP-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Azure Service Bus Data Owner' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-messaging/providers/Microsoft.ServiceBus/namespaces/mybus'

# Event Hub: same pattern
# Azure Event Hubs Data Sender / Data Receiver / Data Owner
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'TELEMETRY-SP-OBJECT-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Azure Event Hubs Data Sender' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-streaming/providers/Microsoft.EventHub/namespaces/myeventhub/eventhubs/iot-telemetry'
Prefer entity-level scope (queue or event hub) over namespace-level scope. A compromised Sender assigned to one queue cannot exfiltrate or inject messages into other queues in the namespace.
Service-Specific Roles in Infrastructure as Code
Define service-specific role assignments alongside the resources that need them. Include the role definition ID as a constant to avoid runtime lookups and ensure the assignment is idempotent.

Teams frequently over-assign Contributor when a service-specific data-plane role would suffice, or under-assign by granting only control-plane Reader without the data-plane counterpart. Both errors lead to either security gaps or broken applications.

# Pitfall 1: Assigning Contributor instead of a data-plane role
# Wrong: gives pipeline full resource management access
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $pipelineSP -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' -Scope $storageScope

# Right: gives pipeline only blob write access
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $pipelineSP -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' -Scope $containerScope

# Pitfall 2: Forgetting that control-plane Reader != data-plane Reader
# Reader grants: list storage accounts, read metadata
# Reader does NOT grant: read blob contents, list blobs
# To read blob contents: need Storage Blob Data Reader

# Pitfall 3: Key Vault Access Policy model blocks RBAC roles
# RBAC roles (Key Vault Secrets User) only work when vault uses RBAC model
Get-AzKeyVault -VaultName $vaultName | Select-Object EnableRbacAuthorization
# If False: roles are ignored; use Access Policies or switch model

# Pitfall 4: AKS RBAC Writer at cluster scope = full cluster write
# Scope to namespace for proper team isolation:
$nsScope = "/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups/$rg/providers/Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters/$cluster/namespaces/$namespace"
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamSP -RoleDefinitionName 'Azure Kubernetes Service RBAC Writer' -Scope $nsScope

# Pitfall 5: VM Contributor without VM Login = can restart VM, cannot log in
# This is often intentional for automation SPs — document the decision
# If you need both: assign BOTH Virtual Machine Contributor AND VM User Login
Contributor at resource scope does NOT grant data-plane access to blobs, secrets, or queues.
Reader at resource scope does NOT grant reading blob contents or Key Vault secrets.
Key Vault RBAC roles only take effect when the vault uses the RBAC permission model, not Access Policies.
AKS RBAC Writer at cluster scope gives write access across ALL namespaces — always scope to namespace.
VM Contributor and VM User Login are two separate assignments; neither implies the other.

Service-specific built-in roles extend the generic Owner/Contributor/Reader set with purpose-built, principle-of-least-privilege roles for every Azure service. The critical insight is the control-plane vs data-plane split: Contributor manages a resource but does not read its data; data-plane roles like Storage Blob Data Contributor or Key Vault Secrets User access the contents directly via Entra ID tokens.
Assign data-plane roles to application managed identities instead of Contributor where possible.
Storage data-plane roles (Blob/Queue/Table) eliminate the need for shared keys or SAS tokens.
Key Vault RBAC model replaces Access Policies — prefer it for new vaults.
VM Administrator Login and VM User Login decouple OS access from resource management.
AcrPull on AKS node-pool kubelet identity is the secure pattern for image pulls.
Monitoring Reader is safe to assign broadly; restrict Monitoring Contributor to SRE teams.
Use Get-AzRoleDefinition filtered by service namespace to discover roles before creating custom ones.
